title: Responding to Larry Boggs' Explanation of Tuesday's Confrontation
author: Sam Riley
orientation: A point-by-point rebuttal of Senator Larry Boggs' public defense of his physical confrontation with abolitionists at a Haskell County GOP meeting, arguing that his shifting explanations contradict the video evidence and that SD7 voters should replace him with Warren Hamilton because of his opposition to SB13.
audiences: Oklahoma SD7 voters weighing the runoff
Christian pastors evaluating rebuke and repentance
Abolitionist activists studying tactics and confrontation
Journalists fact-checking the McAlester News Capital statements

questions.question: What incident is being responded to, and who was involved?
questions.audience: Journalists fact-checking the McAlester News Capital statements
questions.answer: At a Haskell County GOP meeting, Senator Larry Boggs physically shoved his primary opponent Warren Hamilton and verbally threatened Daniel Navejas of The Ekklesia of Oklahoma after being challenged on his abortion record and his refusal to support SB13.
questions.quote: At Tuesday's Haskell County GOP meeting, Senator Larry Boggs shoved his opponent Warren Hamilton and made threats against Daniel Navejas of The Ekklesia of Oklahoma.

questions.question: How did Boggs himself describe the confrontation to the press?
questions.audience: Journalists fact-checking the McAlester News Capital statements
questions.answer: Boggs told the McAlester News Capital that he was simply inviting Hamilton to step outside for a quieter conversation and framed the criticism as out-of-state 'sleazy attacks' from someone who recently moved from Texas.
questions.quote: I said let's go outside and talk without disturbing this meeting… Where I come from, offering to 'take it outside' is an invitation to stand down. My opponent just recently moved here from Texas, so maybe these kinds of sleazy attacks work in Austin, but Oklahomans stand up for themselves.

questions.question: Why does the author find Boggs' 'let's talk outside' explanation implausible?
questions.audience: Oklahoma SD7 voters weighing the runoff
questions.answer: Because Boggs' actual words on video were a confrontational challenge, not an invitation to dialogue, and the voters can decide for themselves which interpretation matches the footage.
questions.quote: His exact words were, "step outside and you square right up." The voters of Senate District 7 can decide whether this language invites civil dialogue or whether it sounds like an invitation to brawl.

questions.question: How does Sam Riley use Scripture to challenge Boggs' professed Christianity?
questions.audience: Christian pastors evaluating rebuke and repentance
questions.answer: By citing Proverbs to argue that a wise believer welcomes rebuke rather than retaliating, contrasting Boggs' defensive tirade and 'screw you' with the biblical posture toward correction.
questions.quote: That word says, "Better is open rebuke than hidden love (Proverbs 27:5)," "A fool despises his father's instruction, but whoever heeds reproof is prudent (Proverbs 15:5)," and "A rebuke goes deeper into one who has understanding than a hundred blows into a fool (Proverbs 17:10)."

questions.question: How did the Boggs campaign characterize the abolitionists at the meeting?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activists studying tactics and confrontation
questions.answer: The campaign called them a 'violent mob' of 'thugs' who allegedly surrounded Boggs and shouted 'baby killer' and other insults, comparing the experience to his post-Vietnam reception.
questions.quote: he claims a "violent mob" of "thugs" surrounded him to call him "baby killer" and other "vile insults" like the people who said the same thing to him after returning from Vietnam.

questions.question: What does the article say the video Boggs himself shared actually proves?
questions.audience: Journalists fact-checking the McAlester News Capital statements
questions.answer: The video Boggs released contradicts his own claims by showing no insults from the crowd, his own campaign manager physically restraining him, and Boggs being the one who approached and threatened Navejas.
questions.quote: No one in the video called him a baby killer, used any vile insults, or was violent. In fact, Boggs' campaign manager is seen physically restraining Senator Boggs and Boggs is the only person using vile language.

questions.question: How did Warren Hamilton respond during the heated moment, according to the author?
questions.audience: Oklahoma SD7 voters weighing the runoff
questions.answer: Hamilton attempted to de-escalate by appealing to Boggs' campaign manager about the political optics and urging him to take responsibility for getting Boggs out of the situation.
questions.quote: Hey man, we probably disagree wholeheartedly on just about everything. But a sitting senator getting western is not gonna look good for anybody, all right? You take care of him and you follow him around. You got a responsibility. You take care of him, okay?

questions.question: How does the author answer claims that abolitionists 'provoked' Boggs?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activists studying tactics and confrontation
questions.answer: He argues calling for repentance is a normal part of abolitionist witness and that any wise person, especially a Christian, should welcome rebuke rather than treat it as provocation.
questions.quote: A Christian telling another Christian to repent should not be provocative. To the wise, rebuke is welcome. It gives a person an opportunity to evaluate themselves and realign their thinking and actions to the mind of Christ.

questions.question: What does the article identify as the real reason to vote Boggs out, beyond the confrontation?
questions.answer: His refusal to support SB13, the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act, which the author argues will result in another 5,000 Oklahoma children being killed in 2021.
questions.quote: The main reason he should be opposed is because his refusal to support The Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act helped seal the fate of 5,000 more Oklahoma babies who he is supposed to represent.

questions.question: What metaphor does the author use to describe pro-life Republican promises on abortion?
questions.answer: He compares the establishment's promise to end abortion to a carrot dangled in front of a horse that never actually gets eaten, accusing pro-life politicians of perpetually deferring justice.
questions.quote: For the Republican pro-life establishment, the end of abortion is a carrot on a string and Oklahoma voters are the horse. *The horse never gets the* *carrot*.

questions.question: What concrete electoral action does the article ask SD7 voters to take?
questions.audience: Oklahoma SD7 voters weighing the runoff
questions.answer: Vote Boggs out in the August 25th run-off and replace him with Warren Hamilton to dramatically improve the chances of passing SB13 in the next legislative session.
questions.quote: In the August 25th run-off, SD7 voters have an opportunity to replace someone who does not intend to abolish abortion soon with a righteous man who will strive to see abortion ended immediately.

questions.question: How does the author frame Oklahoma's collective responsibility for abortion?
questions.audience: Christian pastors evaluating rebuke and repentance
questions.answer: He treats the state as corporately guilty for the killing of 5,000 image-bearers each year and calls both legislators and citizens to repent of either sanctioning or tolerating the practice.
questions.quote: The State of Oklahoma is guilty of allowing the murder of 5,000 image bearers of God annually. We need to repent.
